story:
Beneath a gnarled oak whose branches spread like mourning fingers, the Grim Reaper stood, his tattered cloak swallowing the thin dawn. From the kitchen window, Frank watched him and clutched a chipped coffee mug as if its warmth could hold the empty house together. The scythe flashed with a cold, hard sheen. Frank willed the figure to leave.

Mary’s voice came soft and firm: Give him what he wants. Frank twisted his hearing aid and pretended he hadn’t heard. He didn’t want to hear. He sipped the coffee, grimaced at the bitter fake sugar Mary always insisted on, and tried to skewer the Reaper with a glare. Mary spoke again, louder, teasing him for being deaf as bread, patient as ever. Frank shouted back that he’d heard. He hadn’t.

He wanted the Reaper to be cruel so he could hate him. But even from here, Death looked tired, like someone who did a hard job and took no joy in it. Mary said he wasn’t a bad man—just waiting, just working. Frank muttered that waiting could still feel unkind.

Micah’s coming today, Mary reminded him. Frank left the window for the barn-red table. His knees creaked like rusty hinges, and when he shooed at the shadow now lingering in the hallway, his knuckles caught the table’s edge. A thin cut opened and bled more than it should. We are old, Mary chuckled, then gentled: We’ve lived a long time. Maybe it’s time. Frank didn’t like the word. He dabbed at the blood and snapped back. She scolded him, and he laughed, but the laugh died quick. It isn’t about liking, she said. No matter how tight we hold on, at last we have to let go.

Let go. Everyone said it: kids, pastor, doctors. It sounded simple until you had to unclench a hand shaped by a lifetime. Mary asked if he remembered those early letters, the lines he wrote every time. Love you to the moon and back, he guessed, and she laughed. He knew what she meant: the promise he’d made never to let go, never to leave. You don’t have to keep that promise now, she said. You can let go. His voice broke. I don’t know what to do without you. You’ll watch the kids and the grandkids, she said, watch football, play a little golf. Just enjoy what’s left. Promise me.

Frank nodded. For seventy-eight years he’d been an oak for their family, weathering storms. But Mary had been his roots, feeding his strength, holding him upright. She had given him so much. This time the strong thing to do was to release. In his mind he felt her hand. He squeezed once, then did the hardest thing he’d ever done. He let go.

The Reaper waited. Okay, Frank said. You can take her. Just… don’t leave me too long without her. Death dipped his head and was gone.

Micah opened the front door. You ready, G-Dad? Frank wiped his eyes and stood. Let’s go say goodbye to your Granny. On the threshold, he took one last look. Mary wasn’t here. She lay unresponsive at the hospital. He’d held on longer than he should have. Now he stepped into the morning light with a steady heart and closed the door.
